packages.name: numpy
packages.synopsis: Package numpy is a small, dependency-free n-dimensional array library for Go modeled on the core of Python's NumPy.
packages.doc:
Package numpy is a small, dependency-free n-dimensional array library for Go
modeled on the core of Python's NumPy. It provides a single dense array type,
NDArray, holding float64 values, together with creation helpers, shape
manipulation, broadcasting element-wise math, axis reductions, basic linear
algebra, and boolean masking.

# The NDArray model

An NDArray stores its elements in one flat []float64 slice. The logical view
of that buffer is defined by three pieces of metadata:

  - shape: the length of the array along each axis.
  - strides: how many flat elements to advance to move one step along each
    axis. Strides are expressed in elements, not bytes.
  - ndim and size: cached number of axes and total element count.

Newly constructed arrays are contiguous in row-major (C) order. Some
operations return views that share the underlying buffer without copying:
Transpose permutes axes by permuting strides, and Slice selects a sub-range
by adjusting the offset and shape. Because views alias their parent's data,
writing through a view is visible in the parent. Operations that must produce
independent data (arithmetic, reductions, Reshape, Ravel, Copy) always return
a fresh contiguous array.

# Creation

FromSlice, FromData and FromNested build arrays from existing values. Zeros,
Ones, Full, Arange, Linspace, Eye and Identity generate arrays. Reshape
re-views the same data under a new shape (with one optional inferred -1 axis).

# Indexing and manipulation

At and Set read and write single elements by multi-index (negative indices
count from the end). Transpose, Ravel/Flatten, Slice, Concatenate and Stack
reshape and combine arrays.

# Broadcasting

Binary element-wise operations broadcast their operands following NumPy's
rules. Shapes are aligned from the trailing axis; two dimensions are
compatible when they are equal or one of them is 1, and a size-1 dimension is
virtually repeated (implemented with a zero stride, so no data is copied
during the broadcast itself). For example a (3, 1) array and a (1, 4) array
broadcast to (3, 4). Incompatible shapes panic.

# Element-wise math and reductions

Add, Sub, Mul, Div, Pow and the comparison operators broadcast two arrays;
the *Scalar variants combine an array with a constant. Neg, Abs, Sqrt, Exp,
Log, Sin and Cos map a function over every element. Sum, Mean, Max, Min, Std,
Var and Prod reduce the whole array to a scalar, while SumAxis, MeanAxis,
MaxAxis, MinAxis, StdAxis and ProdAxis reduce along one axis with an optional
keepdims flag.

# Linear algebra and masking

Dot computes a 1-D dot product or a 2-D matrix product, and MatMul multiplies
two matrices. The comparison operators return boolean masks encoded as 1.0 /
0.0 float64 arrays; MaskSelect gathers the elements where a mask is set and
Where chooses element-wise between two arrays.

# Errors

The library is deterministic. All shape and dimension validation failures
panic with a message prefixed by "numpy:". This keeps the arithmetic API free
of error returns so operations can be chained; wrap calls with recover if you
need to trap invalid usage.

types.name: NDArray
types.doc:
NDArray is an n-dimensional, row-major array of float64 values.

The element data is held in a single flat slice. The logical view of that
data is described by shape (the length along each axis) and strides (the
number of flat elements to step to advance one position along each axis).
A freshly created array is always contiguous in row-major (C) order, but
views produced by Transpose or slicing may be non-contiguous.

All shape-validation failures panic with a clear message. This keeps the
arithmetic API free of error returns and lets operations be chained. Use
recover if you need to trap these panics.

funcs.name: Arange
funcs.doc:
Arange returns a 1-D array of evenly spaced values in the half-open interval
[start, stop) advancing by step. It panics if step is zero.

funcs.name: Concatenate
funcs.doc:
Concatenate joins arrays along an existing axis. All arrays must have the
same shape except along the concatenation axis.

funcs.name: Diag
funcs.doc:
Diag has two behaviors mirroring NumPy. Given a 1-D array of length n it
returns the n-by-n matrix with that array on its main diagonal and zeros
elsewhere. Given a 2-D array it returns its main diagonal as a new 1-D array.
It panics for arrays of other dimensionality.

funcs.name: Eye
funcs.doc:
Eye returns an n-by-m matrix with ones on the k-th diagonal and zeros
elsewhere. A positive k is above the main diagonal, negative below.

funcs.name: FromData
funcs.doc:
FromData creates an array from a copy of data laid out in row-major order
for the given shape. It panics if len(data) does not match the shape size.

funcs.name: FromNested
funcs.doc:
FromNested creates an array from an arbitrarily nested slice of float64
(for example []float64, [][]float64, [][][]float64). The nested value must
be rectangular; a ragged structure panics.

funcs.name: FromSlice
funcs.doc: FromSlice creates a 1-D array from a copy of the given values.

funcs.name: Full
funcs.doc: Full returns a new array of the given shape filled with value.

funcs.name: Identity
funcs.doc: Identity returns the n-by-n identity matrix.

funcs.name: Linspace
funcs.signature: func Linspace(start, stop float64, num int, endpoint bool) *NDArray
funcs.doc:
Linspace returns a 1-D array of num evenly spaced values. When endpoint is
true, stop is the final value; otherwise stop is excluded. It panics if
num is negative.

funcs.name: Ones
funcs.doc: Ones returns a new array of the given shape filled with 1.

funcs.name: OnesLike
funcs.doc: OnesLike returns a one-filled array with the same shape as a.

funcs.name: Stack
funcs.doc:
Stack joins arrays along a new axis. All arrays must have identical shapes.
The result has one more dimension than the inputs, inserted at axis.

funcs.name: Where
funcs.doc:
Where returns an array selecting from x where mask is non-zero and from y
otherwise. All three are broadcast together.

funcs.name: Zeros
funcs.doc: Zeros returns a new array of the given shape filled with 0.

funcs.name: ZerosLike
funcs.doc: ZerosLike returns a zero-filled array with the same shape as a.

methods.name: Abs
methods.doc: Abs returns the element-wise absolute value of a.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add returns a + b with broadcasting.

methods.name: AddScalar
methods.doc: AddScalar returns a + s.

methods.name: All
methods.doc: All reports whether every element is non-zero.

methods.name: AllClose
methods.doc:
AllClose reports whether two arrays have the same shape and all elements
differ by no more than tol in absolute value.

methods.name: Any
methods.doc: Any reports whether any element is non-zero.

methods.name: Arccos
methods.doc: Arccos returns the element-wise inverse cosine (in radians) of a.

methods.name: Arcsin
methods.doc: Arcsin returns the element-wise inverse sine (in radians) of a.

methods.name: Arctan
methods.doc: Arctan returns the element-wise inverse tangent (in radians) of a.

methods.name: Arctan2
methods.doc:
Arctan2 returns the element-wise arc tangent of a/b choosing the quadrant
from the signs of both arguments, with broadcasting. It mirrors NumPy's
arctan2 and math.Atan2 (a is the "y" argument, b is "x").

methods.name: Argmax
methods.doc:
Argmax returns the flat index (in logical row-major order) of the first
maximum element. It panics on an empty array.

methods.name: Argmin
methods.doc:
Argmin returns the flat index (in logical row-major order) of the first
minimum element. It panics on an empty array.

methods.name: Argsort
methods.doc:
Argsort returns the indices that would sort a flattened copy of the array in
ascending order, as a 1-D float64 array. The sort is stable, so equal
elements keep their original relative order.

methods.name: At
methods.doc:
At returns the element at the given multi-index. Negative indices count
from the end of each axis.

methods.name: BroadcastTo
methods.doc: BroadcastTo returns a contiguous copy of a broadcast to shape.

methods.name: Cbrt
methods.doc: Cbrt returns the element-wise cube root of a.

methods.name: Ceil
methods.doc: Ceil returns the element-wise ceiling of a (least integer >= x).

methods.name: Clip
methods.doc:
Clip returns a copy of a with every element limited to the range [min, max].
Values below min become min and values above max become max. It panics if
min > max.

methods.name: Copy
methods.doc: Copy returns a new contiguous array holding a copy of the data.

methods.name: Cos
methods.doc: Cos returns the element-wise cosine of a.

methods.name: Cosh
methods.doc: Cosh returns the element-wise hyperbolic cosine of a.

methods.name: Cross
methods.doc:
Cross returns the cross product of two 1-D arrays, mirroring NumPy's
numpy.cross for 1-D inputs. Each operand must have length 2 or 3, with any
missing third component treated as zero:

  - two 2-vectors yield the scalar z-component a[0]*b[1] - a[1]*b[0],
    returned as a 0-dimensional array whose single value is the result;
  - any combination involving a 3-vector yields a 3-vector.

It panics if either operand is not 1-D or has a length other than 2 or 3.

methods.name: Cumprod
methods.doc:
Cumprod returns a 1-D array of the running cumulative product of the elements
of a taken in logical row-major order. The result has the same number of
elements as a.

methods.name: Cumsum
methods.doc:
Cumsum returns a 1-D array of the running cumulative sum of the elements of a
taken in logical row-major order. The result has the same number of elements
as a.

methods.name: Data
methods.doc:
Data returns a copy of the array's elements in logical row-major order.
The result is always contiguous regardless of the array's internal layout.

methods.name: Diagonal
methods.doc:
Diagonal returns the k-th diagonal of a 2-D array as a new 1-D array. A
positive k selects a diagonal above the main one, a negative k below it. It
panics if a is not 2-D.

methods.name: Diff
methods.doc:
Diff returns the first discrete difference of a flattened 1-D copy of a, that
is out[i] = a[i+1] - a[i]. The result has one fewer element than a; an array
with 0 or 1 elements yields an empty result.

methods.name: Div
methods.doc: Div returns the element-wise quotient a / b with broadcasting.

methods.name: DivScalar
methods.doc: DivScalar returns a / s.

methods.name: Dot
methods.doc:
Dot computes a dot product. For two 1-D arrays it returns a scalar array of
shape (1). For two 2-D arrays it is equivalent to MatMul. Other combinations
panic.

methods.name: Equal
methods.doc: Equal reports whether two arrays have the same shape and identical elements.

methods.name: EqualMask
methods.doc: EqualMask returns a mask that is 1 where a == b, with broadcasting.

methods.name: EqualScalar
methods.doc: EqualScalar returns a mask that is 1 where a == s.

methods.name: Exp
methods.doc: Exp returns the element-wise base-e exponential of a.

methods.name: ExpandDims
methods.doc:
ExpandDims returns a copy of a with a new axis of length 1 inserted at the
given position. axis may range from 0 to ndim inclusive; negative values
count from the end (where -1 appends after the last existing axis).

methods.name: Expm1
methods.doc: Expm1 returns the element-wise value of exp(x)-1, accurate for x near zero.

methods.name: Flatten
methods.doc: Flatten is an alias for Ravel; it returns a contiguous 1-D copy.

methods.name: Flip
methods.doc:
Flip returns a new contiguous array with the order of elements reversed along
the given axis. Negative axis values count from the last axis. It panics if
axis is out of range.

methods.name: Floor
methods.doc: Floor returns the element-wise floor of a (greatest integer <= x).

methods.name: Greater
methods.doc: Greater returns a mask that is 1 where a > b, with broadcasting.

methods.name: GreaterEqual
methods.doc: GreaterEqual returns a mask that is 1 where a >= b, with broadcasting.

methods.name: GreaterScalar
methods.doc: GreaterScalar returns a mask that is 1 where a > s.

methods.name: Hypot
methods.doc:
Hypot returns the element-wise sqrt(a*a + b*b) computed without undue
overflow, with broadcasting.

methods.name: Less
methods.doc: Less returns a mask that is 1 where a < b, with broadcasting.

methods.name: LessEqual
methods.doc: LessEqual returns a mask that is 1 where a <= b, with broadcasting.

methods.name: LessScalar
methods.doc: LessScalar returns a mask that is 1 where a < s.

methods.name: Log
methods.doc: Log returns the element-wise natural logarithm of a.

methods.name: Log10
methods.doc: Log10 returns the element-wise base-10 logarithm of a.

methods.name: Log1p
methods.doc:
Log1p returns the element-wise natural logarithm of 1+x, accurate for x near
zero.

methods.name: Log2
methods.doc: Log2 returns the element-wise base-2 logarithm of a.

methods.name: MaskSelect
methods.doc:
MaskSelect returns a 1-D array of the elements of a where mask is non-zero,
in row-major order. The mask must be broadcast-compatible with a; it is
broadcast to a's shape before selection.

methods.name: MatMul
methods.doc:
MatMul returns the matrix product of two 2-D arrays. The receiver has shape
(m, k) and b has shape (k, n); the result has shape (m, n). It panics on a
dimension mismatch or non-2-D input.

methods.name: Max
methods.doc: Max returns the largest element. It panics on an empty array.

methods.name: MaxAxis
methods.doc: MaxAxis reduces a by maximum along the given axis.

methods.name: Maximum
methods.doc: Maximum returns the element-wise maximum of a and b with broadcasting.

methods.name: Mean
methods.doc: Mean returns the arithmetic mean of all elements. It panics on an empty array.

methods.name: MeanAxis
methods.doc: MeanAxis reduces a by mean along the given axis.

methods.name: Median
methods.doc:
Median returns the median of all elements. For an even number of elements it
returns the average of the two middle values. It panics on an empty array.

methods.name: Min
methods.doc: Min returns the smallest element. It panics on an empty array.

methods.name: MinAxis
methods.doc: MinAxis reduces a by minimum along the given axis.

methods.name: Minimum
methods.doc: Minimum returns the element-wise minimum of a and b with broadcasting.

methods.name: Mod
methods.doc:
Mod returns the element-wise remainder of a/b with broadcasting, computed as
math.Mod (the result has the sign of the dividend a).

methods.name: Mul
methods.doc: Mul returns the element-wise product a * b with broadcasting.

methods.name: MulScalar
methods.doc: MulScalar returns a * s.

methods.name: Ndim
methods.doc: Ndim returns the number of dimensions (axes) of the array.

methods.name: Neg
methods.doc: Neg returns the element-wise negation of a.

methods.name: Norm
methods.doc:
Norm returns the Frobenius (L2) norm of a: the square root of the sum of the
squares of all elements. For a 1-D array this is the Euclidean vector norm.

methods.name: NotEqualMask
methods.doc: NotEqualMask returns a mask that is 1 where a != b, with broadcasting.

methods.name: Outer
methods.doc:
Outer returns the outer product of two arrays. Both operands are flattened to
1-D; for inputs of length m and n the result is an (m, n) matrix with
out[i,j] = a[i] * b[j].

methods.name: Percentile
methods.doc:
Percentile returns the q-th percentile of all elements, with q in the range
[0, 100]. It uses linear interpolation between the two nearest ranks, the
same as NumPy's default ("linear") method. It panics on an empty array or if
q is outside [0, 100].

methods.name: Pow
methods.doc: Pow returns a raised element-wise to the power b, with broadcasting.

methods.name: PowScalar
methods.doc: PowScalar returns a raised element-wise to the power s.

methods.name: Prod
methods.doc: Prod returns the product of all elements.

methods.name: ProdAxis
methods.doc: ProdAxis reduces a by product along the given axis.

methods.name: Ptp
methods.doc:
Ptp returns the "peak to peak" range of a, that is Max minus Min. It panics
on an empty array.

methods.name: Quantile
methods.doc:
Quantile returns the q-th quantile of all elements, with q in the range
[0, 1]. It is equivalent to Percentile(q*100).

methods.name: Ravel
methods.doc: Ravel returns a contiguous 1-D copy of the array in row-major order.

methods.name: Reciprocal
methods.doc: Reciprocal returns the element-wise reciprocal 1/x of a.

methods.name: Reshape
methods.doc:
Reshape returns a new array with the same data viewed under a new shape.
Exactly one dimension may be -1, in which case it is inferred from the total
size. The data is copied to contiguous order first, so the result is always
independent of the receiver.

methods.name: Roll
methods.doc:
Roll returns a new array the same shape as a with its flattened elements
shifted circularly by shift positions (positive shifts move elements toward
higher indices). The elements are taken in logical row-major order, rolled,
and reshaped back, matching NumPy's roll with no axis argument.

methods.name: Round
methods.doc:
Round returns the element-wise rounding of a to the nearest integer, with
halves rounded to the nearest even value (banker's rounding), matching
NumPy's default round behavior.

methods.name: SearchSorted
methods.doc:
SearchSorted returns the leftmost index where v could be inserted into a to
keep it sorted in ascending order. The receiver must be a 1-D array that is
already sorted; the result lies in the range [0, len]. It panics if a is not
1-D.

methods.name: Set
methods.doc: Set stores v at the given multi-index. Negative indices count from the end.

methods.name: Shape
methods.doc: Shape returns a copy of the array's shape.

methods.name: Sign
methods.doc:
Sign returns the element-wise sign of a: -1 for negative values, 0 for zero,
and 1 for positive values. A NaN input yields NaN.

methods.name: Sin
methods.doc: Sin returns the element-wise sine of a.

methods.name: Sinh
methods.doc: Sinh returns the element-wise hyperbolic sine of a.

methods.name: Size
methods.doc: Size returns the total number of elements in the array.

methods.name: Slice
methods.doc:
Slice returns a view selecting a contiguous range [start, stop) along each
axis. Provide one Range per axis. A zero Range value (Start and Stop both 0)
is treated as the full extent of that axis. Negative Start/Stop count from
the end. The result shares data with the receiver.

methods.name: Sort
methods.doc:
Sort returns a new 1-D array containing all elements of a in ascending order.
The array is flattened in logical row-major order before sorting, mirroring
NumPy's sort of a flattened array. NaN values sort to the end.

methods.name: Sqrt
methods.doc: Sqrt returns the element-wise square root of a.

methods.name: Square
methods.doc: Square returns the element-wise square of a (x*x).

methods.name: Squeeze
methods.doc:
Squeeze returns a view-independent copy of a with all axes of length 1
removed. If every axis has length 1 the result is a 1-D array of length 1.

methods.name: Std
methods.doc: Std returns the population standard deviation of all elements.

methods.name: StdAxis
methods.doc: StdAxis reduces a by population standard deviation along the given axis.

methods.name: StdDDof
methods.doc:
StdDDof returns the standard deviation of all elements using the given delta
degrees of freedom; it is the square root of VarDDof(ddof).

methods.name: Strides
methods.doc: Strides returns a copy of the array's strides (in elements, not bytes).

methods.name: String
methods.doc: String renders the array's shape and data for debugging.

methods.name: Sub
methods.doc: Sub returns a - b with broadcasting.

methods.name: SubScalar
methods.doc: SubScalar returns a - s.

methods.name: Sum
methods.doc: Sum returns the sum of all elements.

methods.name: SumAxis
methods.doc: SumAxis reduces a by summation along the given axis.

methods.name: T
methods.doc: T is shorthand for a full transpose (reverse of all axes).

methods.name: Tan
methods.doc: Tan returns the element-wise tangent of a.

methods.name: Tanh
methods.doc: Tanh returns the element-wise hyperbolic tangent of a.

methods.name: Trace
methods.doc:
Trace returns the sum of the elements on the main diagonal of a 2-D array.
It panics if a is not 2-D.

methods.name: Transpose
methods.doc:
Transpose returns a view with the axes permuted. With no arguments the axis
order is reversed. Otherwise axes must be a permutation of 0..ndim-1. The
result shares data with the receiver (it is a view, not a copy).

methods.name: Trunc
methods.doc: Trunc returns the element-wise truncation of a toward zero.

methods.name: Unique
methods.doc:
Unique returns a new 1-D array of the distinct element values of a, sorted in
ascending order.

methods.name: Var
methods.doc: Var returns the population variance of all elements (dividing by N).

methods.name: VarDDof
methods.doc:
VarDDof returns the variance of all elements using the given delta degrees of
freedom: the sum of squared deviations from the mean is divided by
(N - ddof). ddof=0 gives the population variance (like Var) and ddof=1 gives
the unbiased sample variance. It panics if N-ddof <= 0.

types.name: Range
types.doc:
Range describes a half-open [Start, Stop) selection along one axis for Slice.
If both fields are zero the whole axis is selected. Negative values count
from the end of the axis.

funcs.name: R
funcs.doc: R is a convenience constructor for a Range.

examples.name: NDArray (MaskSelect)
